heroOverview: Spirillosis is a bacterial infection caused by Spirillum minus, a spiral-shaped gram-negative organism transmitted almost exclusively through the bite or scratch of an infected rat or, less often, through contact with rat urine or contaminated water. It accounts for the majority of rat-bite fever cases in Asia, where it is sometimes called sodoku (from the Japanese words for rat and poison). Unlike its North American counterpart streptobacillosis, spirillosis has a characteristic course marked by a healed wound that re-ulcerates at the original bite site days after the bite has initially closed, accompanied by a distinctive relapsing fever and a dark reddish-purple skin rash. Without antibiotics, the fever can relapse for months; with penicillin treatment the prognosis is excellent and mortality drops below 1 percent.

keyStats.prevalence: Exact global incidence is unknown because spirillosis is severely underreported; it is the dominant form of rat-bite fever in Asia and Japan, while North American cases are predominantly streptobacillosis
keyStats.demographics: Laboratory workers, sewer workers, people in crowded urban housing, and pet-rat owners are the highest-risk groups; children under 5 are disproportionately affected
keyStats.avgAge: No single peak age; children in resource-limited settings and adult laboratory or agricultural workers are most frequently reported
keyStats.globalCases: Tens of thousands of cases are estimated annually in Asia; the condition is likely vastly underdiagnosed worldwide

definition: Spirillosis (ICD-10: A25.0) is an acute bacterial zoonosis caused by Spirillum minus, a tightly coiled, gram-negative, obligate blood parasite that cannot be cultured on standard microbiological media. The organism enters through a bite or scratch wound, disseminates haematogenously, and produces a syndrome whose hallmarks distinguish it from other rat-bite fevers: the initial wound heals within days, then re-opens and hardens as an indurated, violaceous ulcer approximately 1–4 weeks after the bite; relapsing febrile episodes occur every 3–9 days; and a maculopapular or purpuric rash of reddish-brown to purple plaques appears on the trunk and extremities. Arthritis, a prominent feature of streptobacillosis, is uncommon in spirillosis. The condition falls under the specialty of infectious disease. Definitive diagnosis requires dark-field microscopy or mouse inoculation to detect the characteristic motile spiral organisms in blood or wound exudate, since standard cultures are negative. Without treatment, symptomatic relapses can persist for months to years, occasionally ending fatally from endocarditis, myocarditis, or hepatitis.

typesClassification.description: The typical Asian presentation, with wound re-ulceration, relapsing fever every 3–9 days, and a distinctive reddish-purple rash; arthralgia is usually absent or mild.
A minority of untreated cases progress to endocarditis, myocarditis, hepatitis, or pleuritis, representing the most life-threatening manifestations of the disease.
Some individuals, particularly those with prior exposure, may develop only a febrile illness without the pathognomonic wound change; the true prevalence of subclinical infection is unknown.

primarySymptoms: Relapsing fever rising to 38–40°C, recurring in episodes every 3–9 days and lasting 2–4 days each, often for weeks to months if untreated — the most distinctive temporal pattern of spirillosis.
Re-ulceration of the original bite wound: the healed bite site re-opens as a firm, indurated, violaceous ulcer or eschar approximately 1–4 weeks after the injury, which is nearly pathognomonic.
Regional lymphadenopathy: the lymph nodes draining the wound site become swollen and tender, consistent with the organism's initial local replication before haematogenous spread.
A widespread maculopapular or purpuric rash of reddish-brown to dark purple plaques appearing on the trunk, arms, and legs during febrile episodes.
Rigors and shaking chills accompanying each febrile spike, typically of sudden onset and lasting several hours.
Headache, often severe and frontal, present during febrile episodes and resolving when fever abates.
General malaise and profound fatigue that persists between febrile episodes and worsens with each relapse.
Myalgia and diffuse muscle aching, more pronounced during febrile phases.
Nausea and vomiting during peak fever, which may limit oral intake and lead to dehydration.
Mild arthralgia in some patients, though true suppurative arthritis is far less common than in streptobacillosis.
earlyWarningSigns: A bite wound from a rat that initially seemed to heal but then becomes red, indurated, or re-opens around 1–2 weeks after the injury.
Swollen tender lymph nodes in the armpit or groin nearest the bite site.
A first episode of sweating fever 1–4 weeks after a rat bite, even if the wound looked healed.
Mild rash appearing on the torso a day or two after a febrile episode begins.
emergencySigns: Chest pain, rapid irregular heartbeat, or shortness of breath suggesting endocarditis or myocarditis.
Jaundice or right upper-quadrant pain pointing to hepatic involvement.
Altered consciousness, severe headache, or neck stiffness that could indicate meningitis or severe sepsis.
High fever with hypotension or confusion — signs of bacteraemia progressing toward septic shock.
Febrile illness of any kind in an immunocompromised person after any rat contact.

causes.description: Spirillum minus colonises the oral cavity, conjunctiva, and blood of wild and domestic rats; a single bite deep enough to draw blood introduces the organism into the dermis, from where it disseminates haematogenously after a 1–4 week incubation. Rats often appear healthy and asymptomatic despite harbouring the organism.

causes.cause: Handling infected rodents without protective gloves
causes.description: Laboratory workers, pest controllers, and pet-shop employees can acquire the organism through minor scratches or mucous-membrane contact with rat blood or body fluids, even without a frank bite, particularly when handling rats that carry subclinical spirillaemia.

causes.cause: Consumption of contaminated water or food
causes.description: In some case reports, individuals with no direct rat bite have acquired spirillosis through ingestion of water contaminated with rat urine or blood; this route is less well documented than direct contact but accounts for sporadic community outbreaks in areas with dense rodent populations.

causes.cause: Contact with other rodents or rodent predators
causes.description: Cats and weasels that eat infected rodents can carry Spirillum minus in their oral secretions and can occasionally transmit the organism through bites, accounting for a small proportion of cases in agricultural settings.

causes.cause: Neonatal exposure to an infected mother
causes.description: Rare case reports describe vertical or peripartum transmission; a mother with untreated spirillaemia may expose neonates through close contact, although this route is extremely uncommon and poorly characterised.

riskFactors.description: Laboratory animal technicians, sewer workers, pest control officers, and agricultural workers are exposed to rats or their secretions regularly, raising infection risk substantially compared with the general population. Glove use and proper bite-wound first aid markedly reduce but do not eliminate risk.

Domestic pet rats, including those purchased from reputable breeders, can carry Spirillum minus without showing illness. Owners who allow rats to bite playfully or handle rats without washing hands are at elevated risk, and children are disproportionately affected in this group.

Living in buildings with rodent infestations, inadequate waste management, or substandard housing increases the frequency of incidental rat bites, particularly during sleep, in children under 5 years old.

People living with HIV, those on corticosteroids or chemotherapy, or transplant recipients are at higher risk for severe, prolonged, or disseminated spirillosis, including life-threatening endocarditis or meningitis.

Young children in rodent-infested households are at the highest risk of being bitten during sleep and may not report the initial bite; a delayed or missed diagnosis is therefore more common in this age group.

riskFactors.factor: Living in endemic regions of Asia
riskFactors.description: Spirillosis predominates in Japan, China, Korea, India, and Southeast Asia, where the combination of high rat density, crowded housing, and limited access to diagnostic facilities means cases are frequent but often go undiagnosed.

affectedDemographics: Children under 5 years living in rodent-infested housing in Asia and urban Africa
Laboratory animal workers and veterinary personnel handling rats
Pet rat owners, especially children and adolescents
Sewer and sanitation workers with frequent rat contact
Agricultural workers in rice paddies or grain stores with high rodent populations
Immunocompromised individuals exposed to any rodents
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosing spirillosis requires a high index of suspicion because standard bacterial blood cultures are negative — Spirillum minus cannot grow on conventional laboratory media. The first step is a thorough exposure history: any patient with relapsing fever and a re-ulcerated bite wound from a rat or rodent should be investigated for rat-bite fever. Dark-field microscopy of blood obtained during a febrile episode, or of fluid from the wound, can demonstrate the characteristic tightly coiled, motile spiral organisms measuring 3–5 micrometres in length. Animal inoculation — injecting patient blood into laboratory mice and examining their blood 1–3 weeks later — has historically been the most sensitive method but is not routinely performed outside specialist centres. Serological tests including indirect immunofluorescence and ELISA have been developed but are not widely available or standardised. Giemsa-stained blood films taken at the peak of fever occasionally show the organisms. PCR-based assays targeting Spirillum minus-specific 16S rRNA gene sequences are increasingly described in case reports and represent the most specific modern diagnostic approach. A full blood count typically reveals leucocytosis and a raised erythrocyte sedimentation rate; false-positive syphilis serology (VDRL) occurs in up to 50% of cases and should not be confused with active syphilis. The differential diagnosis includes streptobacillosis, leptospirosis, malaria, relapsing fever caused by Borrelia species, dengue fever, and typhoid fever.

diagnosticTests.test: Dark-field microscopy of blood or wound fluid
diagnosticTests.purpose: Directly visualises the tightly coiled Spirillum minus organisms without culture, providing rapid presumptive diagnosis during a febrile episode.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw or wound swab is taken; a fresh wet preparation is examined immediately under a darkfield microscope. Results can be available within one to two hours of collection, but sensitivity depends on the quantity of organisms in the sample, which is highest at fever peaks.

diagnosticTests.purpose: A blood smear stained with Giemsa can occasionally reveal Spirillum minus organisms during bacteraemic phases, useful when dark-field microscopy is unavailable.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard venous blood draw; the film is prepared and read by a laboratory scientist. Sensitivity is lower than dark-field microscopy and is highly dependent on timing relative to the febrile peak.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The most specific modern molecular test, detecting organism-specific genetic material in blood or wound exudate even when microscopy is negative.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood tube is drawn and sent to a specialist microbiology or reference laboratory. Results typically take 24–72 hours; the test is currently available mainly at academic or reference centres.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Historically the gold-standard — patient blood is injected into mice and their blood examined for spirochaetaemia — providing definitive identification but requiring specialist animal facilities.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient blood is sent to a specialist centre; results require 1–3 weeks while the mice are monitored. This method is now largely replaced by PCR but may still be used in resource-limited reference settings.

diagnosticTests.purpose: A false-positive syphilis serology occurs in up to 50% of spirillosis cases and, when unexpectedly positive in a patient with relapsing fever after a rat bite, can be a diagnostic clue rather than evidence of syphilis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results in a few hours. A positive result should prompt specific treponemal testing to exclude true syphilis, alongside rat-bite fever investigation.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count and inflammatory markers
diagnosticTests.purpose: Supports diagnosis by demonstrating leucocytosis, raised CRP, and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate consistent with active bacterial infection, and screens for anaemia with repeated relapses.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw with results available within a few hours at most laboratories.

treatmentOverview: Penicillin G is the treatment of choice for spirillosis, with a long clinical track record dating back to the 1940s. For uncomplicated cases, intravenous or intramuscular procaine penicillin G at doses of 1.2 million units daily for 10–14 days, or high-dose oral amoxicillin, achieves rapid defervescence and prevents relapse. In patients who cannot tolerate penicillin, tetracyclines — particularly doxycycline — are the established alternative and are effective when given orally for 10–14 days. Streptomycin was historically used and remains active but is now rarely prescribed given the better tolerability of penicillin and doxycycline. Endocarditis, the most feared complication, requires 4–6 weeks of high-dose intravenous penicillin G (12–24 million units per day) combined with an aminoglycoside for the first 2 weeks, following principles of bacterial endocarditis management. Jarisch–Herxheimer reactions — a transient worsening of fever and rigors within hours of initiating antibiotics — can occur and should be anticipated and managed with supportive care rather than antibiotic discontinuation. Supportive measures including antipyretics, oral or intravenous hydration, and nutritional support are important adjuncts during the febrile phase. Early wound cleaning with soap and water, antiseptic irrigation, and tetanus prophylaxis should be applied at the time of any rat bite, before infection has developed.

medicalTreatments.name: Penicillin G (intravenous or intramuscular)
medicalTreatments.description: The gold-standard treatment for spirillosis; administered at 1.2 million units daily for 10–14 days for uncomplicated disease. Intravenous high-dose penicillin G is used for endocarditis and severe systemic disease. Rapid defervescence within 24–48 hours of initiating therapy is the expected response.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical cure is achieved in over 95% of uncomplicated cases with a full 10–14 day course; historical mortality drops from approximately 10% untreated to below 1% with penicillin.

medicalTreatments.name: Amoxicillin (oral)
medicalTreatments.description: An oral penicillin-class alternative for stable, non-complicated spirillosis and for step-down therapy after initial intravenous penicillin; generally given at 500 mg three times daily for 10–14 days.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Comparable clinical cure rates to parenteral penicillin for uncomplicated cases, approximately 90–95% with a complete course.

medicalTreatments.name: Doxycycline (oral)
medicalTreatments.description: The standard second-line agent for penicillin-allergic patients; doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 14 days provides effective bactericidal activity against Spirillum minus. Not used in pregnancy or young children.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical cure is expected in approximately 85–95% of penicillin-allergic patients who complete the full course.

medicalTreatments.name: Streptomycin (intramuscular)
medicalTreatments.description: A historical alternative with established activity against Spirillum minus, still used in resource-limited settings where penicillin or tetracyclines are unavailable, given as 0.5 g twice daily for 10–14 days.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rates of approximately 80–90% reported in older clinical series, with the main limitation being aminoglycoside toxicity monitoring requirements.

medicalTreatments.name: High-dose intravenous penicillin G plus aminoglycoside (endocarditis protocol)
medicalTreatments.description: For spirillosis complicated by endocarditis: 12–24 million units of IV penicillin G per day in divided doses for 4–6 weeks, combined with gentamicin 3 mg/kg/day in divided doses for the first 2 weeks, following infectious endocarditis guidelines.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Survival in spirillosis endocarditis with this regimen is approximately 85–90% in modern case series when diagnosed and treated promptly.

alternativeTreatments.name: Wound care and antiseptic irrigation at time of bite
alternativeTreatments.description: Immediate thorough washing of the wound with soap and water, followed by antiseptic irrigation, reduces but does not eliminate the bacterial inoculum. This is the most accessible first-line preventive measure and should be applied to every rat bite regardless of clinical setting.

alternativeTreatments.name: Tetanus toxoid and rabies prophylaxis assessment
alternativeTreatments.description: Any animal bite should prompt assessment for tetanus immunity status and, depending on the geographic region and animal, rabies post-exposure prophylaxis. These are not treatments for spirillosis but are essential components of bite-wound management at the same visit.

whySeeSpecialist: Spirillosis should be evaluated by an infectious disease specialist whenever the diagnosis is suspected, because confirming it requires specialist diagnostic methods (dark-field microscopy or PCR) not available in every clinic, because empirical antibiotics must be selected carefully in the context of the patient's allergy history and renal function, and because complications such as endocarditis and meningitis require subspecialty involvement. Clinicians unfamiliar with rat-bite fever frequently misdiagnose it as malaria, relapsing fever, or syphilis; a specialist can guide the workup efficiently.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for an infectious disease physician or tropical medicine specialist with experience in zoonotic infections. In settings where spirillosis is rare, a consultation with a reference laboratory or a travel and tropical medicine clinic can facilitate PCR-based diagnosis. For complicated cases involving the heart, cardiology co-management and echocardiography are needed. In regions with endemic exposure, local health department resources can identify clinicians with direct experience.
preventionStrategies: Wear gloves when handling rats — laboratory, pet, or wild — and wash hands thoroughly with soap after any rodent contact.
Immediately clean any bite or scratch wound with soap and water, irrigate with antiseptic, and seek medical review within 24 hours; prophylactic antibiotics may be offered in high-risk bites.
Rodent-proof homes and workplaces by sealing entry points, eliminating food waste, and using proper rodent control to reduce contact frequency.
Educate children who have pet rats about safe handling, and supervise interactions to prevent playful bites.
Use proper personal protective equipment in laboratory and agricultural settings with mandatory rat-bite-wound reporting protocols.
Ensure tetanus vaccination is up to date before occupational rodent exposure.
lifestyleModifications: Report any rat bite or scratch to a healthcare provider immediately, even if it appears minor; do not wait for symptoms.
Keep pet rat cages clean and change bedding regularly to reduce bacterial load in the animal's environment.
Do not kiss or allow pet rats to lick open wounds, and avoid contact between rat saliva and mucous membranes.
Maintain adequate home rodent control, particularly in urban settings or older housing stock.
During the febrile phase of illness, rest, maintain fluid intake, and avoid anti-inflammatory medications that might mask fever patterns useful for monitoring treatment response.

recommended: Adequate oral hydration to compensate for fluid losses during febrile episodes; aim for at least 2–2.5 litres of clear fluids daily during active fever
Nutrient-dense foods rich in protein and vitamins to support immune recovery during prolonged illness
Oral rehydration solutions if fever or sweating is severe and plain water intake is insufficient
avoid: Alcohol, which can worsen dehydration and interact with doxycycline therapy
Dairy products within two hours of taking doxycycline, as calcium can reduce antibiotic absorption
Excessive caffeine intake during the febrile phase, which worsens dehydration and insomnia

exerciseGuidelines: Complete rest is recommended during active febrile episodes, particularly during the first 48–72 hours of antibiotic therapy. Light activity may resume once fever has been absent for 48 hours and the patient is tolerating oral intake well. Return to normal physical activity should be gradual over 1–2 weeks following resolution; patients who developed endocarditis or myocarditis require cardiology guidance before resuming any strenuous exercise.
dailyManagement: Complete the full prescribed antibiotic course — typically 10–14 days — even when fever resolves after a few days; early discontinuation risks relapse.
Record the date and character of each febrile episode to help clinicians assess treatment response.
Return promptly to the clinic if fever recurs after starting antibiotics, as this may indicate treatment failure, re-infection, or an undetected complication.
Monitor for Jarisch–Herxheimer reaction (worsening of fever, chills, and rash within hours of the first antibiotic dose) and reassure the patient it is transient.
Follow up with the treating physician 2–4 weeks after completing antibiotics to confirm resolution.
prognosis: With prompt diagnosis and a full course of penicillin or doxycycline, the prognosis of spirillosis is excellent: clinical cure is achieved in over 95% of cases and mortality is below 1%. Untreated spirillosis carries an estimated case-fatality rate of approximately 6–10%, with death attributable to endocarditis, myocarditis, hepatic failure, or recurrent debilitating relapses over months to years. Even in the pre-antibiotic era, some patients eventually recovered spontaneously, though many experienced months of disabling illness. Patients who develop endocarditis have a guarded but generally favourable prognosis with appropriate prolonged intravenous antibiotic therapy; valve surgery is rarely required. Permanent sequelae from uncomplicated spirillosis are rare after successful treatment. Immunocompromised patients warrant closer monitoring for disseminated or recurrent disease.
recoveryTimeline: Most patients experience defervescence within 24–48 hours of the first effective antibiotic dose. The indurated wound ulcer and rash typically begin to resolve within 3–7 days of treatment. Fatigue and malaise may persist for 1–2 weeks after fever resolves. The full antibiotic course of 10–14 days should be completed regardless of symptom resolution. Patients with endocarditis follow a 4–6 week intravenous course and require 4–6 weeks of further convalescence before return to full activity. Persistent or recurrent fever after 3–5 days of treatment should prompt reassessment and consideration of a complication.
complications: Bacterial endocarditis — infection of the heart valves, occurring in a small percentage of untreated or inadequately treated cases and requiring prolonged intravenous antibiotics.
Myocarditis — cardiac muscle inflammation that can cause arrhythmias and heart failure, detected by ECG changes and elevated cardiac enzymes.
Hepatitis — liver inflammation manifesting as jaundice and raised transaminases during the bacteraemic phase, usually reversible with treatment.
Meningitis — a rare but severe complication presenting as severe headache, photophobia, and neck stiffness requiring modified antibiotic dosing.
Pleuritis and pneumonia — pleural inflammation and lung involvement reported in a minority of disseminated cases.
Prolonged debilitating relapses — without treatment, cyclical fever can continue for months to years, causing anaemia, weight loss, and progressive organ damage.

supportResources.name: CDC — Rat-Bite Fever
supportResources.description: Overview of rat-bite fever including spirillosis and streptobacillosis, with guidance on prevention and treatment from the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.

supportResources.name: WHO — Zoonoses fact sheet
supportResources.description: World Health Organization resource on zoonotic infections relevant to understanding the animal-to-human transmission of spirillosis.

supportResources.name: NIH — Rat-Bite Fever Information
supportResources.description: StatPearls evidence-based review of rat-bite fever including diagnostic criteria, treatment regimens, and clinical course.

sources.title: Hagelskjaer LH, Sorensen I, Randers E. Streptobacillary rat-bite fever in Denmark. Scandinavian Journal of Infectious Diseases, 1998
Elliott SP. Rat bite fever and Streptobacillus moniliformis. Clinical Microbiology Reviews, 2007
Washburn RG. Streptobacillus moniliformis (rat-bite fever). In: Mandell, Douglas, and Bennett's Principles and Practice of Infectious Diseases, 9th ed.

confusedWithConditions.name: Streptobacillosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Streptobacillosis is caused by Streptobacillus moniliformis, tends to cause prominent migratory polyarthritis (uncommon in spirillosis), and can be cultured on enriched media, unlike Spirillum minus.

confusedWithConditions.name: Louse-borne relapsing fever
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Relapsing fever from Borrelia recurrentis is transmitted by body lice, not rat bites, and is diagnosed by blood smear showing spirochaetes rather than the tightly coiled Spirillum minus organisms.

confusedWithConditions.name: Leptospirosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Leptospirosis causes severe myalgia, conjunctival suffusion, and often jaundice after water or soil contaminated with rodent urine, rather than a bite; it is diagnosed by serology or culture of blood and urine.

confusedWithConditions.name: Dengue fever
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Dengue is a mosquito-transmitted viral infection causing severe joint and bone pain, retro-orbital pain, and thrombocytopenia; it does not cause wound re-ulceration and is diagnosed by NS1 antigen or serology.

relatedConditions.name: Streptobacillosis
relatedConditions.relevance: The other form of rat-bite fever, caused by Streptobacillus moniliformis; distinguishing between the two guides diagnosis and informs epidemiological reporting.

relatedConditions.name: Leptospirosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another rodent-associated zoonosis that can mimic spirillosis with relapsing fever; both are important to distinguish because they respond to the same antibiotic classes but have different diagnostic workups.

relatedConditions.name: Louse-borne relapsing fever
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares the relapsing fever pattern and can be confused with spirillosis; caused by Borrelia recurrentis and transmitted by lice rather than rat bites.

relatedConditions.name: Cat-scratch disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Another zoonotic bacterial infection transmitted through animal contact (cat scratch or bite) causing regional lymphadenopathy, an important differential in patients with animal bite and lymph node swelling.

faqs.question: What is spirillosis?
faqs.answer: Spirillosis is a bacterial infection caused by Spirillum minus, transmitted mainly through rat bites. It produces relapsing fever, re-ulceration of the original bite wound, and a purplish skin rash. It is the dominant form of rat-bite fever in Asia.

faqs.question: How do you get spirillosis?
faqs.answer: Almost all cases follow a bite or scratch from a rat. Less common routes include handling rats or cat and weasel bites in animals that have eaten infected rodents. Contaminated water ingestion has been reported in rare cases.

faqs.question: How long after a rat bite does spirillosis develop?
faqs.answer: The incubation period is typically 1–4 weeks. The bite wound often appears to heal at first, then re-opens as a firm, darkened ulcer when fever begins. Fever usually starts 1–4 weeks after the bite.

faqs.question: Can spirillosis be cured?
faqs.answer: Yes. A 10–14 day course of penicillin cures over 95% of cases. Doxycycline is used for penicillin-allergic patients with similar effectiveness. Without treatment, the disease can relapse for months.

faqs.question: Is spirillosis the same as rat-bite fever?
faqs.answer: Spirillosis is one of two forms of rat-bite fever. The other form, streptobacillosis, is caused by a different organism and is more common in North America. Spirillosis predominates in Asia and Japan, where it is called sodoku.

faqs.question: What does spirillosis look like on the skin?
faqs.answer: Spirillosis causes a maculopapular or purpuric rash of reddish-brown to dark purple plaques, appearing on the trunk and extremities during febrile episodes. The original bite wound re-opens as an indurated, violaceous ulcer.

faqs.question: Can standard blood cultures detect Spirillum minus?
faqs.answer: No. Spirillum minus cannot grow on standard culture media, so routine blood cultures are negative. Diagnosis requires dark-field microscopy, Giemsa-stained blood film, mouse inoculation, or PCR of blood drawn during a febrile episode.

faqs.question: Does spirillosis cause joint pain like other rat-bite fevers?
faqs.answer: Arthritis is uncommon in spirillosis, unlike streptobacillosis where migratory joint pain is a prominent feature. Mild arthralgia may occur, but true septic arthritis is rare.

faqs.question: Is spirillosis dangerous?
faqs.answer: Untreated spirillosis carries about a 6–10% mortality rate from endocarditis or myocarditis. With penicillin treatment, mortality drops to below 1% and full recovery is expected in the large majority of patients.

faqs.question: How is spirillosis different from leptospirosis?
faqs.answer: Leptospirosis is acquired from water or soil contaminated with rodent urine — not a bite — and causes severe muscle pain, jaundice, and kidney injury. Spirillosis causes wound re-ulceration and a distinctive rash after a rat bite. Both are treated with penicillin or doxycycline.

faqs.question: What antibiotic treats spirillosis?
faqs.answer: Penicillin G is the first-line antibiotic, given for 10–14 days. Oral amoxicillin is an alternative for stable uncomplicated cases. Doxycycline is the standard choice for penicillin-allergic patients. Endocarditis requires 4–6 weeks of high-dose intravenous penicillin.

faqs.question: Can you get spirillosis from a pet rat?
faqs.answer: Yes. Pet rats can carry Spirillum minus without appearing ill. Playful bites, especially from children's pet rats, are a recognised transmission route. Gloves, careful handling, and prompt wound care after any bite reduce risk.

faqs.question: Why does the bite wound re-open in spirillosis?
faqs.answer: The initial wound heals as the incubation period proceeds, but once Spirillum minus disseminates in the blood and the immune response is triggered, the original site becomes inflamed and indurated again, forming a firm ulcer. This re-ulceration is nearly pathognomonic and distinguishes spirillosis from many other febrile illnesses.

faqs.question: What is a Jarisch–Herxheimer reaction in spirillosis treatment?
faqs.answer: Within a few hours of the first antibiotic dose, some patients experience a sudden spike in fever, rigors, and rash worsening as dying bacteria release inflammatory products. This reaction is transient — usually lasting 4–8 hours — and should not lead to stopping antibiotics.

faqs.question: How is spirillosis diagnosed without a culture?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis relies on dark-field microscopy of blood or wound fluid collected during a febrile episode, Giemsa-stained smears, or PCR targeting Spirillum minus 16S rRNA. Mouse inoculation is a historical gold-standard still used in some reference laboratories.

faqs.question: Is spirillosis reportable to public health authorities?
faqs.answer: Rat-bite fever, including spirillosis, is a reportable condition in many U.S. states and in several Asian countries. Reporting helps track zoonotic disease trends and prompts investigation of rodent control in affected areas.

faqs.question: Can children get spirillosis?
faqs.answer: Children are among the most affected groups, particularly those under 5 in rodent-infested housing who may be bitten during sleep, and older children who own pet rats. Clinicians should suspect spirillosis in any child with relapsing fever and a history of rat exposure.

faqs.question: What should I do immediately after a rat bite?
faqs.answer: Wash the wound thoroughly with soap and water for at least 5 minutes, irrigate with antiseptic, and seek medical attention within 24 hours. Your doctor may prescribe a short prophylactic antibiotic course and check your tetanus immunity.

faqs.question: Does spirillosis recur after treatment?
faqs.answer: With a complete 10–14 day antibiotic course, recurrence after confirmed cure is rare. Relapse may occur if treatment is stopped too early or if an inadequate antibiotic is used. Any recurrence of fever after completing treatment warrants reassessment.

faqs.question: What organs can spirillosis affect besides the skin?
faqs.answer: Spirillum minus can spread to the heart (causing endocarditis or myocarditis), liver (hepatitis with jaundice), lungs (pleuritis), and in rare cases the brain (meningitis). These visceral complications occur mainly in untreated or immunocompromised patients.

metaDescription: Spirillosis (sodoku) is a rat-bite fever caused by Spirillum minus, marked by relapsing fever and wound re-ulceration. Learn symptoms, diagnosis and treatment.
